data.title: Webull: Strong Growth Story, But Valuation Demands Patience
data.description:
Webull Corporation (NASDAQ:BULL) is an American financial services company that focuses on digital investments and offers a trading platform to its customers. BULL emphasizes a seamless user experience and provides brokerage services for trading stocks, options, ETFs, and other instruments.

The company went public on April 11 via a merger with a SPAC. BULL experienced a massive demand for its stock, surging more than 300% in the first few trading days, but has been in a downward channel since then. The stock is down roughly 49% over the past 6 months, indicating that the sell-off may appear overdone. However, I argue that the stock remains expensive, and further consolidation is a possibility.

The stock currently trades at a forward P/E of around 103, highlighting a nearly 9.5x premium versus the sector median of about 11x. The stock may rebound from these levels, but I think the market will wait for management to execute. Therefore, I think Webull is a Hold with a $12 price target over the next 12 months. This represents roughly a 10% upside possibility, but market-like performance rather than outperformance.

Q2 2025: Strong Revenue Growth And High-Demand For Services

On August 28, the company reported Q2 2025 results. This was a strong quarter for the company, highlighting significant revenue growth and strong demand for its services, yet the market met the results with a roughly 7% sell-off over the next trading day.

Webull delivered $131.5 million in revenue, underscoring about a 46% increase on a year-over-year basis. As management highlighted in their presentation, this was driven by significant trading revenue growth, underlining 63% growth versus the same quarter last year, signaling higher volumes.

On the bottom line, the company delivered a net loss of $1.20 per ordinary share. I think this was a key reason that highly contributed to a post-earnings sell-off. On the other hand, Webull focuses heavily on its revenue growth and customer expansion. Therefore, I would not be surprised if the bottom line may remain disappointing over the next several quarters. Furthermore, bottom-line decline supports a bearish stance on the company and may suggest continued consolidation.

On the upside, the financial services company has achieved an all high in customer assets. The figure has reached $15.9 billion, indicating a 64% increase on a year-over-year basis. Management highlighted that registered users grew to 24.9 million, representing 18% growth over the past 12 months, and 4.73 million in terms of funded accounts, up 9% since the same quarter last year.

I think this was a significant quarter for the company, underlining a strong demand for its services. Nevertheless, management announced it has re-entered crypto offerings in the US, and launched crypto services in Brazil and Australia, which could heavily drive customer growth in the near-term future. To that, on September 8th, Webull announced it had entered European markets with the Dutch brokerage launch.

Given a significant focus on revenue and customer growth, I think that the trend of business expansion is likely to continue. However, until the bottom line stabilizes, the company may continue trading at rich valuations, supporting my neutral stance, highlighting that upside may be limited in the near term.

The company is estimated to announce its Q3 2025 results on November 12. Wall Street anticipates $137 million in revenue at the midpoint, and $0.03 in diluted EPS. This would indicate a continuation in the business growth, signaling strong demand for its services.

It would also come in line with the previous quarters' revenue estimates, but would result in a significant bottom-line surprise. I think that beat on the top-line is likely, but I am not sure whether the company can achieve the diluted EPS estimate.

Management has substantial focus on customer growth and business expansion internationally, which I believe may stress the bottom-line figure over the next years. Time will tell, but a turnaround on the diluted EPS figure may be a game-changer for this financial services company, yet I am not sure whether that is the core focus for the company, and whether we will see a bottom-line rebound from this quarter.

Webull Trades At A Premium, And It May Need Time To Rebound

Webull currently trades at a forward P/E of 103.14, which represents a massive premium versus both the sector median of 10.74 and the average company in the S&P 500 at a 23.10 earnings multiple.

It does not necessarily mean that the company cannot trade at this premium further, but I argue that investors may seek other alternatives in the market, that has a more significant market share and trade at a lower earnings multiple, such as Robinhood at a forward P/E of 63.40 and 26.7 million in funded customers. If that happens, this could substantially stress the stock, and downward momentum may continue.

The financial services company trades at a Price/Sales ratio of 10.11, indicating roughly a 3.5x premium versus peers of 2.84. This suggests that the market may have already priced in the future revenue growth. Therefore, even if management continues to execute well, this would not necessarily translate into stock price appreciation or multiple expansion.

On the upside, Webull appears to be a low-leverage business, and that meaningfully contributes to its bullish outlook. The company has about $113 million in total obligations. Regardless, the financial services business company has $1.67 billion in cash and could pay its debt with ease.

Given its capital structure, it appears that Webull is well-positioned to operate in both high and low interest-rate environments. For instance, last quarter the company generated $36.3 million in total interest and dividend income, which supports my thesis that high-yield accounts offer a compelling opportunity to benefit from a high-interest environment.

Webull has been a phenomenal top-line growth story, so far. The company grew revenue by 26.51% over the past 12 months, implying nearly 3.4x outperformance versus peers of 7.70%. This supports a bullish narrative for the company and partially justifies the brokerage firm trading at a premium.

BULL operates at an above-market gross profit margin (TTM) of 78%. This implies a significant outperformance versus the sector median of 60%, supporting an elevated multiple. The net income margin (TTM) of negative 3% balances the bullish outlook and presents a disadvantage versus peers of 23%. However, as I have mentioned, this is mainly due to the company's massive business expansion.

This article highlights that Gen Z starts investing early, with 54% of the survey respondents beginning by the age of 21. I think this underscores a massive demographic tailwind for the company. With more people investing and by doing so at an early age, such financial services disruptors as Webull may appear more favorable than traditional banks. These companies are offering an investing and trading platform at low costs, including a large variety of instruments, including crypto, which plays a significant role when choosing a services provider. This may help to establish a sticky customer base, which could lay the foundation for the company in the years ahead.

Wall Street anticipates $0.11 in diluted EPS for FY2025 and $0.17 for FY2026, implying about 62% bottom-line growth from an FY2025 estimate. I think that this is a reasonable estimate. The financial services provider has major tailwinds tied to demographics and early investors, which may appear to be more favorable for retail investors versus traditional banks, given its low-cost policy and all-in-one trading platform.

Webull aims to heavily expand, including other continents other than North America. If the company succeeds, it could significantly impact both top and bottom line growth over the following years. If we apply a 70x earnings multiple, which could be more than 30% below the current forward P/E, to an FY2026 diluted EPS estimate of $0.17, we come in at my 12-month price target of about $12.

This presents a continued consolidation, but an elevated multiple given double-digit revenue growth and international expansion. I think BULL is likely to present market-like returns, rather than significantly outperform the benchmark. This may appear to be a compelling opportunity for long-term investors who seek exposure to a high-growth market of investing and trading, yet at the current valuation, there appears to be little room for error.

Main Risks And Concerns

There are risks and concerns tied to Webull that could significantly impact the stock price performance in the short term.

The financial brokerage firm currently trades at a substantial premium versus both its peers and the average company in the S&P500, which leaves little room for error. If the company's top-line growth weakens or may be clouded by uncertainties, sentiment could dramatically shift, which could result in continued downward momentum for its stock.

The investing and trading landscape has fierce competition. Although this appears to be a high-growth market, there are several well-known players competing for customers. Such all-in-one companies as Robinhood, or more crypto-focused as Coinbase, create a challenging environment for the company to operate in. If Webull loses market share to competition, this could significantly impact sentiment and stress the stock price further.

Webull is heavily dependent on trading volume. Given its commission-free policy, the financial services provider heavily depends on retail investor sentiment and trading activity. If enthusiasm fades, the company may experience weaker-than-anticipated revenue growth. At this point, it would be challenging to justify the company trading at such a premium.

On the other hand, if none of the above materializes, the stock price could continue trading at such levels. If management can successfully expand into other markets, and that translates to both top and bottom-line growth, the company may rebound from here. Until then, investors should remain cautious and monitor sentiment around this company.

Conclusion: Premium Is High, And Valuation Demands Patience — A Hold

I believe that Webull may appear to be a compelling opportunity for investors who seek exposure in a high-growth market. Investors may have seen the recent success of Robinhood and Coinbase, highlighting massive returns for their investors, but it does not necessarily mean that Webull can mirror their success.

The company trades at a rich valuation, which demands patience. Based on my analysis, although management can deliver a double-digit revenue growth further, it may not lead to stock price appreciation. Premium is high, and investors may seek cheaper alternatives in the market. On the upside, BULL's global expansion appears promising, and that could act as a major tailwind in the years ahead.

Nevertheless, I rate Webull Corporation a Hold with a $12 price target over the next 12 months. This presents 10% upside possibility and market-like returns. I don't think it could significantly outperform the benchmark without a significant top and bottom line growth catalyst. 2 Wall Street analysts forecast a $18.50 price target over the next 12 months, indicating about 70% upside possibility. I don't feel that bullish, but if the company surprises, anything can happen.

data.title: Hims & Hers: Use This Drop Before It's Too Late
data.description:
Hims & Hers (NYSE:HIMS) has experienced a hefty sell-off of ~22% from its highs in recent weeks.

In my last article on the company in May, I rated the company a Buy, highlighting a potential undervaluation when underlying the growth prospects of the telehealth market.

In this article we are going to analyze the recent drop, explore potential risks, revamp the valuation, and prepare a checklist for the next earnings on 11/3/2025.

What Caused The Recent Sell-Off?

Volatility is normal for Hims & Hers. As can be seen nicely in the chart above. In my opinion, the most recent drop can be attributed to three things:

1. Insider Selling

Over the last year, insiders have been constantly selling the stock. Understandably, this is leading to lower investor confidence.

I personally, however, think that the importance of this is highly exaggerated. The CEO, Andrew Dudum, alone is currently still holding ~4.3% of the company.

2. Growth Slowdown

In the last earnings, Hims & Hers declared phenomenal YoY growth of 89% in subscribers and 73% in revenue. While this sounds great, HIMS didn't manage to grow subscribers or revenue QoQ.

According to management, this decline was mainly caused by the off-boarding of some GLP-1 subscribers. And at least on the subscriber front, most of this was offset by robust growth in dermatology, oral weight loss, and daily sexual health offerings.

3. Negative Cash Flow

In Q2, HIMS also declared negative cash flow. This was driven by working capital investments.

The balance sheet, however, remains robust with more than $1.1 billion in cash and short-term investments, caused by a recent convertible debt offering.

Management expects the Free Cash Flow to return to positive levels in the second half of 2025.

Risks To Consider

Continuous insider selling can have a permanent negative impact on investor confidence.

The company also needs their recent investments in marketing, growth, and talent to lead to higher growth once again. Should incremental ROI decline, margins might also erode in the long run. Therefore, the company needs to use the next quarter to prove to its investors that the latest quarter was just a small hiccup and they are able to sustainably get back to growing subscribers, revenue, and average revenue per user.

Although management claims that they expect no significant tariff pressure, this could still create problems, especially when it comes to API (Active Pharmaceutical Ingredient) sourcing and their planned international expansion.

Their increasing focus on international expansion in itself also poses risks. Expanding overseas might cause concentration problems and add complexity to operations and regulations. Maintaining robust U.S. growth is, on the other hand, also essential.

Despite diversifying, the business is still vulnerable to the crowded and competitive GLP-1 market (as can be seen in the revenue drop in Q2) as well as other new treatments; execution risk, therefore, in general is high.

Valuation

To evaluate the company, we first need some general assumptions:

2025 Revenue and Profitability: For 2025, I assumed that HIMS manages to achieve the average of management's FY 2025 guidance. Resulting in revenues of $2.35 billion and adjusted EBITDA of $310 million.

Profitability: The company expects to achieve an adjusted EBITDA of $1.3 billion and revenues of $6.5 billion in 2030, resulting in a margin of 20%.

Considering the current margin of 13% and the target rate for 2030, we can therefore expect the following development:

Financial year 2025(e) 2026(e) 2027(e) 2028(e) 2029(e) 2030(e) 2031(e) 2032(e)
Adj. EBITDA Margin 13.0% 14.5% 16.0% 17.5% 19.0% 20.0% 20.0% 20.0%

Free Cash Flow Conversion: Cash conversion has been at 112% and 118% of adj. EBITDA in 2023 and 2024, respectively. For some additional margin of safety, I assumed a rate of 100% for our analysis.

WACC and Perpetuity Growth: For the WACC, I assumed 10%, which is way below the current rate of ~8%. 3.5% as perpetuity growth also seems fitting considering the very high historical growth rates.

Required Growth

With these assumptions, Hims & Hers needs to grow by ~13% p.a. to justify their current valuation.

Realistic Growth

Like mentioned above, management is expecting to generate revenues of $6.5 billion in 2030. From current levels, this can be translated to an average top line growth rate of 22.6% per year until 2030.

This is also pretty much in line with the expected market growth rates of the telehealth market as a whole, further solidifying management's guidance.

  • Grand View Research: 24.6%
  • Market.us: 22.8%
  • Towardshealthcare.com: 24.7%

By assuming this growth, Hims & Hers could currently be undervalued by up to 61%, according to our DCF.

Checklist For The Next Earnings

Out of the risks and the potential reasons for the sell-off mentioned, I want to see the following things in the Q3 earnings on the 3rd of November:

  • QoQ growth
  • Positive free cash flow
  • Continued high profitability or even improvement
  • No/less insider selling

Conclusion

Considering the growth potential as well as the financials and historical growth, the recent drop seems like a compelling buy opportunity.

With the current valuation, Hims & Hers needs to grow their revenue by approx. 13% per year for the next eight years to justify their current valuation. Should the company manage to grow at the underlying market rate or management's 2030 guidance come to fruition, HIMS is currently undervalued by up to 61%.

I expect the company to improve their free cash flow situation as well as achieve solid quarter-over-quarter growth in Q3 of 2025. This could lead to a price jump, as it would negate most of the reasons for the current drop. That's why I currently rate the company a Strong Buy, and investors who want to participate in the telehealth market should use the stock's current weakness before it eventually gains traction again.

data.title: Dry-Powder Options To Replace XLFT.PR.A Redemption
data.description:
The XAI Octagon Floating Rate & Alternative Income Trust (NYSE:XFLT) announced it will redeem its Series A preferred (XFLT.PR.A). There are only a few months left until maturity, so it's not a huge surprise. The stock has been a great dry-powder asset while delivering a yield of around 7.5% since it was added about 6 months ago to our Income Portfolios. With it going away, we are on the lookout for other dry-powder securities with yields north of 7%.

Since its inception, XFLT.PR.A has been admirably stable amid all the volatility that we have seen over the last few years. This ability to keep its value across the cycle is an important feature of dry-powder assets, and it allows investors to take advantage of developing opportunities elsewhere, such as the current sell-off in the BDC sector.

Recall that XFLT as well as EIC are the lower-octane CLO CEFs with sizable non-CLO Equity exposure, the former being allocated primarily to loans and the latter primarily to CLO Debt. This makes the preferreds of these funds significantly lower risk than the preferreds from funds like OXLC, ECC, OCCI, CCIF and others in the sector. At the same time, the market does not often differentiate among these funds, with preferreds yields typically trading at similar levels.

Before looking at alternatives, let's review why CLO CEF senior securities remain very attractive and why they are a key part of our Income Portfolios.

Why CLO Senior Securities Are Worth A Look

The CLO senior security sector is a niche area of the broader income sector. It includes preferreds and baby bonds issued by investment funds (both CEFs and BDCs) that primarily allocate to CLO Equity and Debt securities.

What makes the sector attractive is not just the risk/reward on offer but also a number of additional factors. Specifically, because CEF and BDC sectors are relatively bifurcated (few income investors [and analysts] are well-versed in both sectors), there are greater relative value opportunities than in other parts of the income market. And because the key risk metric investors use (i.e. asset coverage) is partial and misleading, it's easy for investors to go astray. Both of these factors generate a significant amount of alpha for investors willing to put in the work.

The preferreds and baby bonds of the various CLO Equity funds tend to be overshadowed by their common shares, which deliver incredible yields at an even more incredible volatility. However, for investors who want to acquire income securities with historic price stability and attractive absolute and risk-adjusted yields, senior securities may be more appealing.

Finally, unlike their common share counterparts, baby bonds and term preferreds deliver on their yield in terms of performance (barring a default, naturally). We cannot say the same of the common shares of CLO Equity funds which have tended to deliver performance (measured as the total NAV return) well below their yields.

The performance of CLO Equity common shares has been particularly disappointing this year as the sector has been under pressure from an expected easing cycle by the Fed as well as a wave of loan refinancing, which has pushed spreads tighter. Year-to-date, CLO Equity CEFs are sitting on NAV losses of around 20%.

Over the longer-term what we see is that the common shares of the legacy CLO Equity funds have underperformed their preferred shares. Although the latest leg of underperformance was due largely to discount widening of the funds, driven by distribution cuts, what the charts show is that even on a trend basis, the common shares struggle to overcome their much lower-yielding and boring preferreds.

This is what the performance chart looks like for ECC common and preferred shares.

This is what it looks like for OXLC.

And this is what it looks like for OCCI.

This dynamic of more risk / less reward is not unheard of, but it certainly does run counter to the two generic market mantras that, over the longer term 1) higher-yielding securities will tend to outperform lower-yielding securities and 2) higher-risk securities will tend to outperform lower-risk securities.

For our part, we have recognized these dynamics for some time and have largely avoided the common shares of these funds in our Income Portfolios. It should be said that we don't take the more extreme view that the common shares of these funds are uninvestable, but rather, they are best left to more tactical opportunities.

The CLO Equity CEF senior security subsector has a number of unusual and attractive features. First, nearly all securities have mandatory maturities, even the preferreds (save for 2 exceptions). This significantly lowers their duration, derisks them, and makes them less negatively convex from large changes in interest rates.

Secondly, a number of securities in the sector are monthly payers, which is both attractive for many investors and understates their yield relative to the usual quarterly-paying preferreds due to the additional compounding.

Thirdly, the sector features unusual mandatory redemption features that require the funds to redeem the securities if their asset coverage remains below a given level.

Fourthly, the sector has enjoyed repeated support from fund managers who tend to repurchase shares of their preferreds and baby bonds during drawdowns (otherwise they would not be allowed to pay distributions on common shares). This not only improves the asset coverage of the senior securities but also supports their prices in the market.

Fifthly, being funds, these securities are more predictable in their performance than those of "typical" companies, which might be derailed by fraud, loss of a key customer, supplier issues and other factors typical of the corporate world. Fund companies can certainly struggle, but they will struggle for systemic reasons with few if any idiosyncratic surprises so prevalent in the traditional corporate sector.

Alternatives to XFLT.PR.A

What are the drier-powder alternatives to XFLT.PR.A? To replace the stock, we are happy to remain in the same sector. We are not looking for cash assets, as we tend to view the opportunity cost of giving up an additional 3-4% of spread as too high.

BDC OXSQ bonds remain attractive as the company's portfolio is primarily in first lien loans, with CLO Equity making up 37%. There is also no secured facility ahead of the bonds. The company's 2030 bond OXSQH is trading at a yield of 7.66%.

The CEF EIC is another minority CLO Equity fund, being allocated primarily to CLO Debt. It is funded entirely with preferreds, meaning there are no other credit holders ahead of the preferred shareholders. The 2029 preferred EICC trades at a yield of 7.6%.

Finally, the CLO Equity CEF ECC bonds are senior in the capital structure (the fund also has 3 preferreds), meaning the preferreds will soak up any losses before the bondholders are hit. The 2031 bond ECCW trades at a yield of 8.1%.

CLO fund senior securities remain compelling decent-yield / drier-powder assets in the current market environment.

data.title: This Week In Markets: Earnings, Inflation, And Geopolitical Shifts
data.description:
By Ryan J. Puplava, CMT®, CTS™, CES™

The stock market continued its October rebound with a decisive move higher this week, powered by a combination of upbeat earnings, a cooler-than-expected inflation report, and renewed optimism around U.S.-China trade relations. The S&P 500, Nasdaq Composite, and Dow Jones Industrial Average each closed at fresh record highs by Friday, marking a full recovery from the early-October pullback. Gains were broad-based, with strong participation from cyclical, technology, and financial names, underscoring investor confidence in both corporate and macroeconomic momentum.

This week’s action can be summarized across three dominant themes:

  1. Return of earnings-driven leadership
  2. Macro tailwind from softer inflation and rising rate cut expectations
  3. Growing sense of geopolitical and trade stability.

In addition, I'm keeping a close eye on oil’s rebound this week off key long-term support near $56, which I'll highlight below.

Earnings Season Ignites Sector Rotation and Confidence

Corporate earnings were the primary market driver this week, fueling sector-specific rallies and helping equities reclaim record levels. After several sessions of choppy action earlier in the month, investors responded positively to a string of earnings beats across industrials, consumer discretionary, and technology.

Industrials and Manufacturing Strength

3M (MMM)(+7.7%), Raytheon (RTX)(+7.7%), and Honeywell (HON)(+6.8%) each delivered beat-and-raise results, reinforcing the sector’s reputation as a bellwether for the broader economy. The iShares U.S. Aerospace & Defense ETF (ITA) climbed nearly 2% midweek, aided by strong results from GE Aerospace (GE)(+1.3%) and Danaher (DHR)(+5.9%). Even though Lockheed Martin (LMT) and Northrop Grumman (NOC) gave back modest ground after their own beats, the group as a whole outperformed, suggesting resilient demand for defense and manufacturing capacity amid heightened geopolitical tensions.

Consumer Discretionary and Auto Stocks Roar Back

The consumer discretionary sector also came alive thanks to an eye-catching surge in automakers. General Motors (GM)(+14.9%) raised full-year guidance and downplayed tariff concerns, triggering sympathy gains in Ford (F)(+4.8% Tuesday, +10.3% Friday). Las Vegas Sands (LVS)(+12.4%) added a spark later in the week after beating expectations and boosting its dividend, showing renewed consumer strength in both travel and leisure spending. The group’s rebound, combined with Tesla’s (TSLA)(+2.3%) late-week recovery after its earnings miss, suggested that investors are still favoring companies with credible growth narratives and manageable cost structures.

Tech Earnings and Semiconductors Take the Lead

Technology remained a steady source of leadership throughout the week, though volatility persisted within chipmakers. Advanced Micro Devices (AMD)(+6.6%), Super Micro Computer (SMCI)(+5.5%), and Lam Research (LRCX)(+4.5%) all posted sharp gains, lifting the PHLX Semiconductor Index 2.1% midday Friday. Even after Texas Instruments’ (TXN) rare earnings miss and subdued guidance midweek, semiconductor optimism returned quickly, helped by IBM’s (IBM) endorsement of AMD chips for quantum computing and Alphabet’s (GOOG)(GOOGL) expanded AI infrastructure deal with Anthropic.

The rotation toward tech and industrial names—sectors that benefit from both AI-driven investment and cyclical recovery—reflected renewed risk appetite as the week progressed.

Investment Implication: Earnings breadth is widening beyond the “Magnificent Seven,” signaling a healthier bull market foundation. Continued strength in industrial and semiconductor names may foreshadow a fourth-quarter leadership shift toward cyclicals and infrastructure plays tied to capital expenditure and AI manufacturing demand.

Cooling Inflation Reinforces the Case for Fed Rate Cuts

Friday’s September CPI report confirmed the inflation slowdown investors had been hoping for. Headline CPI rose 0.3% month-over-month (below the 0.4% consensus), while core CPI increased 0.2% versus expectations for 0.3%. The data strengthened the market’s conviction that the Federal Reserve will deliver a 25-basis-point rate cut at next week’s meeting, with futures now fully pricing in an additional cut by December.

Bond yields pulled back modestly in response, boosting equity valuations and extending gains in rate-sensitive sectors such as utilities (+1.5%), real estate (+0.4%), and financials (+1.3%). Bank stocks also gained momentum, with the KBW Regional Banking ETF (KBWR) rising 2.5% earlier in the week and names like Zions Bancorp (ZION)(+4.6%) trading higher after alleviating credit concerns.

The inflation news followed several other encouraging data points. Existing home sales rose 1.5% in September, aided by easing mortgage rates, while S&P Global’s preliminary Manufacturing and Services PMIs both edged higher, reflecting stabilization in business activity. Although University of Michigan’s Consumer Sentiment Index remained subdued at 53.6, persistently high inflation expectations did little to dampen the market’s optimism after Friday’s CPI relief.

From a broader perspective, the cooling inflation trend offers a tailwind to both equity valuations and earnings expectations. A softer rate path could extend the current economic cycle, allowing companies to refinance debt more cheaply and maintain capital spending even as growth moderates.

Investment Implication: The market’s rally now rests on an increasingly dovish Fed outlook. If inflation continues to trend lower, investors could see renewed upside in rate-sensitive sectors—particularly financials, real estate, and utilities—while also supporting high-growth tech valuations heading into 2026.

Trade Optimism and Geopolitical Tensions Set Sector Inflection Points

The week also brought a notable shift in geopolitical tone. What began as renewed trade tensions on Wednesday—after reports that the U.S. might impose software export restrictions on China in retaliation for rare earth curbs—ended with a clear turn toward diplomacy. By Thursday, President Trump announced plans to travel to Asia next week for trade talks with 12 countries, including a meeting with Chinese President Xi on October 30. The agenda is said to include soybeans, rare earth metals, nuclear cooperation, and TikTok, with the President calling the previously proposed 157% tariffs “unsustainable.”

The trade thaw, combined with upbeat corporate results, ignited a strong rally in semiconductors, energy, and materials—three sectors most exposed to global trade flows. The energy sector gained 1.3% Thursday after the Treasury Department imposed sanctions on Russian oil giants Rosneft (OTC:RNFTF) and Lukoil (OTC:LUKOY), pushing crude oil above $61 per barrel for the first time in a month. These developments suggest a possible inflection for the oil market, where tightening supply could offset the drag from slower global growth.

By week’s end, the overall tone between Washington and Beijing appeared more constructive. Markets clearly welcomed this shift, as Apple (AAPL)(+3.9%), Alphabet (+2.9%), and Meta (META)(+2.1%) led a recovery in communication services and consumer tech names. Improved sentiment also helped the Vanguard Mega Cap Growth ETF (MGK) rise 1.1% midday Friday, showing that investors remain eager to buy dips in global-facing growth leaders.

However, the geopolitical backdrop remains fluid. The U.S. is expected to announce new sanctions on Russia, while any setback in China talks could reignite volatility in semiconductors and materials. As such, investors are balancing optimism with caution, recognizing that sector leadership could shift rapidly if trade negotiations falter.

Investment Implication: The potential U.S.-China détente could mark an inflection point for semiconductors, energy, and materials, as improving trade flows and reduced tariff risks favor cyclical global exporters. However, continued geopolitical uncertainty keeps a premium on diversification across both growth and defensive assets. Cleveland-Cliffs' (CLF) climb of 21.5% on Monday showed how the new trade environment is helping domestic production, with the company noting Q3 marked a clear sign of demand recovery for U.S.-made automotive-grade steel.

Chart of the Week

West Texas Intermediate (WTI) crude’s rebound from key long-term support near $56 offered only a brief buying window earlier in the week. The Treasury Department’s sanctions on Rosneft and Lukoil Thursday proved pivotal, reversing October’s short-term downtrend and reigniting momentum. WTI now faces several technical hurdles — first, the 50-day moving average, followed by the series of lower highs that have formed since June, and finally, the 200-day moving average overhead.

The technical bounce off $56 was justified, and the sanctions news has provided some initial fuel for the move. However, a sustained bullish trend will require upward revisions in global and U.S. growth expectations, as crude oil typically lags broader economic recoveries. A further sign of a potential inflection point would be a break above the 14-day RSI level of 60, a threshold WTI hasn’t surpassed since June.

Looking Ahead

The week ended on a high note, with the S&P 500 and Nasdaq Composite hitting all-time highs and the Russell 2000 outperforming thanks to renewed risk appetite. Broad market participation, combined with a cooler inflation print and solid corporate earnings, helped reinforce confidence in the U.S. economy despite the ongoing government shutdown.

Next week’s focus will turn to the Federal Reserve’s policy decision and the next round of mega-cap earnings, including Amazon (AMZN), Apple, and Meta. With the government shutdown nearing a potential resolution and trade talks set to resume in Asia, the market enters a pivotal period where both policy clarity and earnings execution will determine whether this breakout sustains through year-end.

For now, investors appear to have regained their footing. The combination of improving macro data, dovish monetary expectations, and resilient earnings power suggests that the fourth quarter could see continued strength—so long as inflation trends and trade diplomacy cooperate.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: DHY: Limited Growth Potential Due To High Interest Rates
data.description:
Overview

Credit Suisse High Yield Bond (NYSE:DHY) operates as a closed end fund that aims to provide exposure to a diverse range of income-producing securities. The fund has an inception dating back to 1998, meaning we are approaching 30 years' worth of operating history to reference. The fund has total operating expenses of 2.54%, but this is a bit misleading because it includes other expenses related to interest. Excluding these additional fees, DHY's actual management fee sits at 1.31% of assets. Income-focused funds can be an efficient way to hedge against market downturns, but these funds also have their own set of weaknesses that investors should be cautious of.

Looking at the performance over the last twelve months, we can see that DHY's share price has declined by about 6.56%. As the sentiment around the debt market shifts, the fund's share price has failed to hold onto any positive momentum. When including all distributions that were paid out to shareholders, the total return jumps up to 2.3% over the same time frame. DHY now offers investors a starting dividend yield of approximately 9% and issues payouts on a monthly basis. Although the fund's reporting indicates that distributions are supported by earnings, I am still cautious about the overall sustainability of payouts.

Additionally, DHY trades at the higher end of its price-to-NAV range over the last ten years. Therefore, it may not be the most ideal time to accumulate right now. The recent reporting also indicates that NAV growth has been limited, which may be attributed to the generous distributions and the higher interest rate environment. As long as interest rates remain elevated, the overall appeal of the fund is limited. So let's start by taking a look at the underlying strategy that DHY implements to generate its earnings.

Fund Strategy

According to the latest fact sheet, DHY has total net assets of $225.2M that are spread across 231 different holdings. The fund has the primary goal of seeking a high current income through a diverse range of bonds and other debt investments. DHY also provides a small amount of exposure outside of the US. US securities make up over 90% of assets, while about 8.5% of assets are allocated to regions in Western Europe. The fund allocates the majority of its assets to high-yield bonds, with the following breakdown as follows:

  • High Yield Bonds: 81.48%
  • Senior Loans: 13.18%
  • Asset-Backed Securities: 4.52%
  • Equities: 0.55%

To avoid concentration risks, DHY allocates its assets across a wide range of industries. For instance, the portfolio is most heavily allocated to the Basic industry at 16.33% of assets. The basic industry refers to companies that are involved in the production of fundamental goods and materials. This is followed by exposure to Capital Goods and Technology securities, which account for 12.89% and 11.67% of assets, respectively. The combination of these top three industries makes up more than 40% of total assets, so investors should anticipate that these industries will have the greatest impact on DHY's earnings and daily price movements.

Looking at the top issuers within the fund, we can see a mix of private and public companies. The only public companies on the list are Northern Oil and Gas (NOG) and First Quantum Minerals (OTCPK:FQVLF). Every other position in the top holdings is a private company, which may be attractive for investors that want to diversify away from public equities. However, we must remember that DHY maintains these positions through income instruments, so investors should limit their expectations around the fund's ability to deliver capital appreciation.

To demonstrate this, I pulled in a performance chart over the last decade. As we can see below, DHY's share price has declined by about 13.45% over the last ten years. The fund has essentially failed to participate in the market rallies over the last decade. When including all distributions that were paid out, the total return jumps to 122% over the same period. Therefore, investors should anticipate that most of their returns will come from DHY's ability to continue paying out a high rate of distributions over time.

Valuation

Since DHY is structured as a closed-end fund, the price can trade at a difference from the actual value of the underlying assets. As a result, DHY now trades at a discount to NAV of approximately 3.29%. For reference, DHY traded at an average discount to NAV of 6.35% over the last five years. The red line on the chart below is the current discount to NAV pricing in relation to its ten-year historical range. As we can see, DHY trades at the higher end of its range, so this may not be an ideal time to accumulate shares.

To measure the internal performance of the fund, I wanted to assess the latest semi-annual report for 2025. Net investment income amounted to $0.09 per share, while net realized losses landed at $0.08 per share. Assuming similar performance over the remainder of the year, net investment income is on track to be on par with its prior years. However, net investment income isn't strong enough to support payouts on its own, so the fund is reliant on net realized gains to bridge the gap in earnings.

Since the report is dated through the end of April, the net realized loss of $0.08 per share may not be an accurate reflection. The market rallied after April, so I suspect that the fund has been able to capitalize in some capacity. The biggest issue is that the fund hasn't been able to recover from the large net realized loss experienced back in 2022. As a result, the NAV growth has been fairly limited over the last few years. For reference, here are the year-end NAV amounts:

  • 2020: $2.39 per share
  • 2021: $2.55 per share
  • 2022: $2.00 per share
  • 2023: $2.03 per share
  • 2024: $2.21 per share
  • 2025 (semi-annual): $2.13 per share

Risk Profile

As part of the fund's operating strategy, DHY implements leverage in an effort to amplify its earnings. The use of leverage currently amounts to 28.56% of assets, which I consider to be an aggressive use of leverage in this higher interest rate environment. Despite the Fed initiating a 25 basis point cut in September, the federal funds rate still remains near the higher end of its ten-year range. As we can see below, this has prevented DHY's share price from maintaining enough momentum to get back to its pre-pandemic levels.

A higher cost of borrowing means that the fund may need to pay out higher interest expenses, which negatively impacts the operating spread between the cost of debt and the yield collected from securities. So as long as rates remain elevated, I believe that DHY's growth potential will be fairly limited. Additionally, the higher interest rates mean that the fund may be impacted by the risk of borrower defaults. For instance, S&P Global estimates that the rate of leveraged loan defaults can increase as high as 3.5% by March of 2026.

To support the higher dividend yield, DHY allocates the majority of its portfolio to securities with lower credit ratings. For reference, a credit rating below a BBB is considered to be below investment grade. These are typically riskier securities because borrowers may not have consistent cash flows or may be more reliant on debt financing to fuel growth. The breakdown by credit rating is as follows:

  • Investment Grade (AAA - BBB): 2.63%
  • Below Investment Grade (BB - D): 95.43%
  • Not Rated: 1.95%

Dividend Sustainability

As of the latest declared monthly distribution of $0.0155 per share, the current dividend yield sits around 9%. If we take this monthly distribution and multiply it by twelve months, we get an estimated annual payout amount of $0.186 per share. Referring back to the same semi-annual report, we can see that DHY has an inconsistent history of generating earnings that exceed payout amounts. For reference, here are the year-end annual earnings amounts over the last few years:

  • 2020: $0.05 per share
  • 2021: $0.35 per share
  • 2022: ($0.36) per share
  • 2023: $0.22 per share
  • 2024: $0.37 per share
  • 2025 (semi-annual): $0.01 per share

As we can see, DHY has demonstrated that it is capable of generating a sufficient level of earnings when market conditions allow. 2024's earnings were also strong enough that I believe there's a good chance that payouts remain consistent over the next twelve months. However, the dividend history clearly indicates that payouts maintained a downward trend from 2015 to 2020. I believe that the fund would benefit from a distribution cut because it would allow a greater amount of NAV growth and reduce the pressure of needing to create a large level of earnings.

Lastly, the reporting indicates that distributions are typically fueled by net investment income. Therefore, most of the distributions received from DHY will be classified as ordinary income, which has the greatest tax burden for investors. So based on your specific tax bracket, the amount of capital invested, and desired income level, a position in DHY would be best utilized within a tax-advantaged account to offset these potential tax implications.

Takeaway

In conclusion, I believe that DHY's overall appeal is limited at this time. The fund trades at the higher end of its historical price-to-NAV range, and growth prospects are limited in this higher interest rate environment. Since the fund utilizes a heavy amount of leverage, the operating spread of the fund may be limiting its growth potential. Additionally, the risk of defaults remain a threat to the fund's returns in the near term. Although distributions have remained steady in recent years, I believe that the fund would benefit from a small dividend cut, which would allow a higher level of NAV growth to occur.

data.title: Integra Resources: Leveraging 1600 RC Drilling And Surging Gold Prices
data.description:
Introduction and Investment Thesis

I am rating Integra Resources Corp. (NYSE:ITRG) as a buy, driven by the 16,000 meters (M) reverse circulation [RC] and sonic drilling methods. This historical mine dump (backfill material) has been regarded as uneconomic low-grade gold-mineralized waste. As such, Integra has completed the 13,000 m process, and it is now in the process of upgrading its mining method to 16,000m which will offer an improved mining process to achieve an economic outcome. I will explain this in detail below to show how this upgraded mining method is economical. But just to mention a bit about this upgrade, Integra has completed a 13,000 m drilling program and is now installing this new 16,000 m drilling program to improve the economic value of the Florida Canyon mine.

Among the economic values is that upgrading RC is set to deliver quality samples, minimal contamination, and fast production, among other benefits. These benefits circle back to cost reduction, which I think is now possible, making the previous low-grade waste economical to mine. Adding to the picture that the gold price has hit an all-time high it adds optimism in mining this low-grade gold mineralized waste at low cost while the price of end product, which is gold, is high.

Additionally, upgrading to this new mining method has catalyzed market optimism, with its stock return at 190.48% compared to the SP500’s at 14.08%.

I will now explore this in more detail to explain my angle on why I believe the Florida Canyon 16,000 mining program is a bullish driver for Integra.

Company Brief

Integra Resources Corp. is a producer of precious metals focused on acquiring, exploring, and developing mineral properties in the Great Basin of the Western United States. The company is primarily focused on gold and silver deposits, which are produced in key mining projects such as the DeLamar Project in Southwestern Idaho and the Wildcat and Mountain View Projects in western Nevada and Idaho. The company has a portfolio of early-stage exploration projects in Idaho, Nevada, and Arizona. It was incorporated in 1997, and it is currently headquartered in Vancouver, Canada.

Digging Deeper

I will first delve into the optimism brought in by the price of gold before I get to the new mining method. I mentioned above that one reason Integra is seeing economic value from the Florida Canyon dump mine is the price of gold. The historical grade estimates ranged between 0.28 and 0.34 g/t Au between 1980 and the 1990s. Now, after the evaluation of the dump, it is estimated that it contains mineable mineralized material with an oxide Au grade ranging between 0.11 and 0.25 g/t oxide Au with a total volume of 34 to 56 million tonnes. This is a diminished mineable mineralized material, but it is important to consider the current price of gold. When this landfill was considered uneconomical, the price of gold was around $325 to $450 per ounce. However, the price has gained significantly above $4,300 per ounce for the first time in history.

Still on the price of gold, one thing to note is that the Florida Canyon mine has a mining life cycle of around 6 to 9.5 years. I think during this lifecycle the mine will be going on, if the price of gold maintains its current price, this will continue to be a profitable endeavor. This is because Integra is selling its gold per ounce at around $3,332. If the price of gold goes below this level, it means that Integra profit margins are likely to shrink. Therefore, going by the estimation that in the next three years, central banks, especially from emerging markets, are expected to continue increasing their demand for gold, I think the current price levels will be sustained.

Given that the price of gold is expected to continue increasing, Integra is elevating its mining from 13,000 m to 16,000 m. This elevation in mining capability is cost-effective from the RC drilling program. It is estimated that the process is about 25%-40% compared to diamond drilling for the same depth. Therefore, optimizing it translates to an improved efficiency which explains its strong 2025 guidance. Florida Canyon is estimated to produce around 70,000 to 75,000 ounces using this 16,000 m RC, which is expected to have the lowest mining cost.

Recent research shows that the RC program optimizes mining through improved accuracy, fast production, and reduced waste generation, which altogether leads to reduced cost of mining by around 30%. It is worth noting that this cost reduction is also due to the sonic drilling, given that it is 10 times faster than conventional drilling in lithologies.

As such, this technology enhances sample quality by delivering undisturbed core samples with accuracy, with less than 1% deviation in the planned hole profile. With Integra expanding in-situ resources around existing mine open pits, sonic drilling comes in to add the economical aspect. Sonic drilling will offer an accurate geological assessment to reduce the amount of drill spoils and cleanup requirements. The whole point here is the reduction of cost, which comes to around 30%.

Financials and Valuation

Integra has started recording revenues after the end of the pre-revenue exploration stage, which ended in 2023. The mining operations at the Florida Canyon Mine have rekindled earnings, indicating that it is a lucrative mine. The revenues have risen from $30.35 million in 2024 to the trailing $148.45 million. By the end of 2025, Integra’s revenues may hit $244.3 million, which represents a 703.62% increase.

According to analysts’ estimates, the revenue for the year 2026 is expected to decrease by 24.73%, which is partly driven by the end of 13,000 m RC. As such, I am still optimistic about Integra because the company is spending approximately 45% of its annual sustaining capital on deploying a functional 16,000 m RC. Approximately $8 million to $10 million in capital is being deployed to expand the project, which supports the new mining method as a major contributor to the cost sustainability of the mine site. Therefore, 2026 will be a capital-intensive year, which I think the company will focus on creating a cost-sustainable mine site.

Integra has become profitable, driven by the gains from the Florida Canyon. In the last five years, it has been in pre-revenue exploration and recorded net losses until Florida Canyon mining began, which has scaled its net profit to the current $14.4 million from a loss of $9.5 million. With this in mind, recall that in 2026 the company will receive an intensive injection of capital to expand its mining program and achieve this reduced cost of mining by upgrading RC. This suggests that the company’s net profit may increase by a modest margin, as the estimated EPS for 2026 is around $0.33, up from $0.25 in 2025. I therefore believe that the optimism of the Florida Canyon mine and new mining method demonstrates an upside that cannot be ignored and is worth leveraging.

To wrap up this section, I will evaluate Integra's valuation using the P/S ratio metric compared to its peers Gold Royalty Corp. (GROY) and Jaguar Mining Inc. (OTCQX:JAGGF). With a P/S ratio of Integra at 2.97x, I consider it undervalued when I compare this to its peers’ average of 26.5x.

Taking this further, I find ITRG’s 2025 estimated revenue growth of 703.6% to be significantly higher than GROY’s 74.7% and JAGGF’s -15.9%. This is an indication that this company is indeed undervalued. While ITRG might experience a revenue dip in 2026 as the DeLamar project permitting could go all the way to 2027, it is not a fundamental weakness but rather a legislative process, which upon completion I expect even stronger returns due to the anticipated efficiencies of the new 1600 RC process. Consequently, I think ITRG deserves a higher valuation, and a PS in the region of 10x-20x is justifiable.

My perspective coincides with the stock's upward momentum, which shows that the market is bridging the value gap, and I think investors should ride this strong momentum. With the new mining technology and the soaring gold prices, I think this trend is likely to continue.

Q3 2025 Earnings Preview: Expectations & Areas of Focus

ITRG is scheduled to release its Q3 2025 report on 12th November 2025. Analysts have a consensus revenue of $37.95 million, which would mark the fourth successive quarter of meaningful revenue. In addition, the consensus EPS stands at $0.10, which signals the company’s consistent profitability.

Notably, when I look at the company’s subsequent quarterly estimates, I am seeing triple-digit growth estimates, which point to solid growth momentum that is kicking in and which aligns with my bullish stance backed by the new mining method and rising prices. For this reason, I think the consensus projections align with my bullish stance.

Based on the company’s recent updates, where the company announced Q3 gold production of 20,653 oz, and based on the 20,265 oz sold (reflecting minor timing differences between production and sales), I expect a significant revenue beat owing to the rising prices.

Assuming the average spot gold price of about $3,320 in Q3 2025, and the minor contribution from silver byproducts, which I find in the region of about 5-10% historically, I estimate revenue of about $68 million. I arrive at this by multiplying the gold sold by the average spot price, plus approximately $2 million from byproducts. My estimates are consistent with the company's uptrend QoQ in revenue, where its revenue has surged from $30.35 million in Q4 2024 to Q2 2025.

For the EPS, I will assume a net profit margin of about 25%, above the Q2 2025 17.4% due to higher revenues and gold prices. Further assuming the outstanding shares of 169 million, I arrive at an EPS of $0.1, which is in line with consensus estimates. In summary, I anticipate ITRG to beat revenue expectations significantly and its EPS to be in line with expectations.

Besides financial metrics, I recommend investors be watchful in two major areas that I think are important. The first point of focus is any updates on the progress of the DeLamar project permitting. Watch for updates on the Mine Plan of Operations [MPO] review under NEPA, timelines for the Draft Environmental Impact Statement [DEIS] and any new government or community support. Positive updates could trigger upward revisions of consensus financial performance and stock price, catalyzing an uptrend.

Another point of interest is the operational advancement at the Florida Canyon mine. Any details on heap leach expansion or equipment upgrades and guidance on 2026 production/cost would be important. Any news on slippage in production or cost spikes could raise concerns about operational reliability, while strong metrics reinforce ITRG’s mid-tier production status.

Investment Risks

Gold Price Volatility

While the price of gold is high, the economic viability of reprocessing low-grade materials is risky because the gold price is inherently volatile. Some factors, such as geopolitical tensions and economic instability, could affect the value of gold instantly, leading to a significant fluctuation from $4,000. This decline in gold price could adversely affect Integra’s revenues, adversely affecting its profitability and increasing operational costs.

Drilling Risk

Even though the Florida Canyon mine uses 16,000 m of RC to focus on the near-surface oxide material and inter-pit areas, if a large material shows a huge discrepancy from the samples evaluated, this could impact production plans. The evaluation of the mine waste indicates a promising intercept, and the program relies on this evaluation to ensure success in continuity and grade of mineralization. If this shows a discrepancy, it could cause a huge ore quality than expected.

Conclusion

Integra’s idea to reprocess this Florida Canyon low-grade material has become an economic endeavor with the new mining method, which is now being improved to 16,000 m RC. Adding the price of gold indicates optimism that it will remain high as demand for it grows. This indicates that the Florida Canyon is currently an ideal mining operation, which is why I am reiterating my bullish rating.

data.title: Kering: Visibility And Valuation Keep Us Neutral
data.description:
Since our last update, From Cars To Couture - Kering Bets On de Meo (Rating Upgrade), we are sure that the Pinault family and the company’s shareholders are pleased, with performance up 92% (Fig. 1). Our last analysis assessed Kering’s (OTCPK:PPRUF)(OTCPK:PPRUY) positives and negatives, building on our long-running coverage. After yesterday’s close, the company posted Q3 sales that, despite already-heightened expectations, still came ahead across every brand. While the bullish thesis focused on Gucci’s turnaround, the broader portfolio also showed a marked inflection, combining an elevated valuation. We continue to be neutral on the equity story.

For our new readers, Kering is a French luxury goods conglomerate with a portfolio centered on Gucci. Following a year of challenges, the company has named a new CEO and split the Chairman and CEO positions. The hire strengthens governance and brings a fresh lens, though the lack of luxury-sector experience and the timing amid soft results have raised questions. A turnaround under De Meo is possible, but profit visibility remains limited. This was the main reason behind our neutral weighted view.

Fig 1

Q3 Results and Our Neutral View

As usual, the company disclosed only Q3 sales, so details are limited; that said, developments have occurred since our last update, including the L’Oréal deal.

Starting with the results, Q3 top-line sales beat expectations across the board, with Gucci retail improving by 10 basis points quarter on quarter. That said, the group's organic sales generation was down by 5%, while Gucci's was down by -14%. Elsewhere, Saint Laurent, Bottega Veneta, and Other Houses reached sales of -4%, +3% and +1%, respectively. As reported by the company, the 5% sales deceleration compared to Q3 2024 is seen as favorable and is a sequential improvement from the -15% reached in Q2 2025.

Source: Kering Q3 sales update - Fig 2

Why are we still neutral?

Gucci contributed over 70% of the total company's core operating profit at one time, and quarterly sales are still down by 14%. The market may call this a turning point, but it is unsettling. The company delivered soft but broadly in-line H1 2025 results, with double-digit declines in sales and profitability. Gucci’s full product refresh under new creative director Demna won’t land until March 2026. While the Q2 2025 weakness was flagged mainly, we believe the Bloomberg consensus for 2026 is still too bullish. With management remaining cautious and LVMH’s Fashion & Leather Goods also underwhelming, we expect the market to revisit its 2026 assumptions for Kering, which could prompt a future lower valuation.

Kering has refreshed its leadership and refocused on restructuring. A quick rebound looks unlikely, given headwinds from the luxury slowdown — particularly in China. Gucci and Saint Laurent remain under pressure. Over the longer term, the equity story still has runway. However, Q4 organic sales generation is expected to align with Q3, with Chinese down high-teens in %. In the meantime, the company anticipates further cost-saving measures across various brands with portfolio rationalization. This will be led by retail store closures and reduced selling space over the years.

Additionally, we are also watching for any strategic pivots or further self-help actions from incoming CEO Luca de Meo, who started on 15 September. However, the market has already pushed significantly, with a stock price appreciation much higher than anticipated.

Over the medium term, we see asset-disposal execution and Valentino’s progress as the two other key drivers of credit spreads. On a positive note, the company announced that it would dispose of its beauty business with L'Oreal. This transaction was valued at €4 billion. The package includes Creed — bought in 2023 for roughly €3.5 billion and rights to develop beauty lines for other Kering labels such as Bottega Veneta, Balenciaga, Alexander McQueen, Pomellato, and Qeelin. Gucci’s beauty license remains with Coty until 2028. We view the transaction as a modest positive for Kering, shoring up the balance sheet that has worried investors this year. While for L’Oréal, it would be relatively small, slightly EPS-accretive, and further reinforce the Luxury division and fragrance portfolio. According to our analysis, this transaction will help Kering to pay off its debt, arriving at 2x net debt/EBITDA. This may also imply a high impairment in Creed acquisition.

Earnings Change and Valuation

There is no 2026 guidance, and with limited visibility in luxury, we are using 2025 sales and margins as the base case for next year. This assessment excludes any impairment Kering may record, which, together with the recent share appreciation, could add short-term pressure to the stock. In our base case scenario, we see Kering sales at €15 billion with a core operating profit of €1.6 billion. Compared to our last update, which forecasted a cash interest payment of €600 million, we now account for the L'Oreal deal and €1.3 billion real estate disposals. Net debt will subsequently improve to €5.5bn compared to €10.5 billion in the Fiscal Year 2024. Therefore, we now see interest payments in the €200 million area. Goodwill impairment is not tax-deductible, and continuing to apply a corporate tax rate of 27%, we derive a net profit of €1 billion. This implied an EPS of €8.1.

Currently, the company trades with a P/E of 42.6x and an EV/Sales of 2.6x. In the luxury industry, peers' companies trade on a P/E and EV/Sales of 25x and 2.1x. We are not presenting EV/EBIT and EV/EBITDA multiples, which are too elevated as restructuring progresses. Blending our valuation methodology aligned with peers' multiple, we derive a value of approximately €27 billion versus a current market cap of €40 billion. Even if this would mean a sell rating, Kering is here to stay, and we prefer to remain neutral.

Risks

Downside risks include: FX changes due to sales in the USA and COGS in Euro terms, brand weakness with failure to sustain elevated brand heat at Gucci, and Chinese cluster sales deceleration. Globally, we also see a macro slowdown and pricing pressure due to luxury fatigue. The company might be subject to 5) lower liquidation from Kering's real estate portfolio, and higher-than-expected rental cost inflation. On the B/S and valuation, we see risks in Valentino's put option exercised and liabilities materialized. Additionally, we are concerned about lower valuation multiples and downtrends in earnings revisions combined with impairment.

Conclusion

Kering’s valuation has run up sharply without support from fundamentals or a positive luxury news flow; we prefer to stay on the sidelines for now.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Markets Weekly Outlook: Trump-Xi Meeting, Earnings And Central Banks
data.description:
By Zain Vawda

Week in review

A rollercoaster week draws to a close for Global Markets. US-China trade talks were driving volatility, and the mixed messages from both parties kept things interesting. Markets also saw harsher sanctions on Russia, which has renewed the geopolitical risk premium when factoring in the Russia/Ukraine situation.

President Trump went back and forth this week, keeping markets guessing as his rhetoric went from diplomatic to combative and back to diplomatic by the end of the week regarding China. A date has now officially been set for a Trump-Xi meeting, which at this stage is set for October 30.

President Trump is heading on an Asia visit this weekend, with a senior US official stating that President Trump will sign economic agreements, including trade and critical minerals, on his trip this weekend.

The news of a Trump-Xi meeting, which markets had expected since the start of the week, led to a rise in equities globally. European stocks closed at record highs.

The S&P 500 and the Nasdaq are set to have their best week in terms of percentage gain since August. Meanwhile, the Dow Jones Industrial Average is heading for its biggest weekly jump since June.

The moves in global stocks were also helped by the cooler-than-expected US CPI data, as well as US earnings.

The current third quarter earnings season is moving very fast, with 143 companies in the S&P 500 having already reported their results. So far, 87% of those companies have surpassed Wall Street's expectations.

Because of these strong results, analysts have raised their overall forecast for S&P 500 earnings growth for the quarter to 10.4% year-over-year, which is a solid improvement from the 8.8% growth that was expected at the beginning of October.

How have the US Dollar and FX Performed?

The US dollar was nearly flat on Friday, steadying after a brief dip following the release of the inflation report, though it remained on track for a small gain for the week.

The euro rose slightly after a survey showed that business activity in the Eurozone, led by the services sector, grew faster than expected in October. The Canadian dollar was slightly weaker but saw a minimal reaction overall.

Meanwhile, the Japanese yen fell to a two-week low despite data released earlier on Friday showing that Japan's core consumer prices remained above the central bank's 2% target, which kept alive expectations for an interest rate hike soon.

Taking a brief look at how commodities ended the week, gold prices reduced their losses on Friday after a slightly cooler-than-expected US inflation report reinforced expectations that the Federal Reserve will cut interest rates next week. Despite this recovery, gold was still set for its first weekly loss in ten weeks.

Meanwhile, oil prices fell slightly as investors grew skeptical about how strongly the Trump administration would enforce its new sanctions on Russia's two largest oil companies.

Even though both oil benchmarks retreated near the end of trading, giving up some of the previous day's large gains, they still finished the week more than 7% higher, marking their biggest weekly jump since mid-June.

The Week Ahead

Given that next week brings a host of central bank decisions, FX markets could have a busy week.

Beyond the data, markets will be focused on the Trump-Xi meeting, Russia-Ukraine developments, as well as renewed tensions between the US and both Venezuela and Colombia. Any signs of US military intervention in Venezuela could add to the risk premium and affect overall market sentiment.

US earnings will also be key, with a host of ‘magnificent 7’ companies all reporting Q3 earnings. Let us take a look at some of the key data releases that could shake markets next week.

Asia Pacific Markets

In the coming week, all attention will be on the US-China trade talks.

The talks begin this weekend in Malaysia with top officials (led by China’s He Lifeng and US Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent) and are expected to lead to the long-awaited face-to-face meeting between President Xi Jinping and President Trump on October 30th in South Korea. This will be their first meeting since 2019, following months of increasing trade fights and threats.

Because the recent language has cooled, and President Trump has spoken optimistically about reaching a "fantastic deal" and even visiting China in 2026, we expect a positive outcome. This deal will likely at least continue the current uneasy trade truce.

However, Trump has kept his options open by suggesting the meeting might not happen. If the meeting does go ahead, it likely means the top officials (He and Bessent) have already agreed on the basic terms of a deal.

Regarding economic news, next week is quieter. The main data release will be China's manufacturing report next Friday, which is expected to show that manufacturing activity is still shrinking.

The Bank of Japan (BoJ) is expected to keep its interest rate at 0.5% on October 30th. Even though the BoJ's board members still disagree on policy, the majority is not ready to change course yet.

I believe that inflation has been firmly rising and the economy is holding up well despite challenges like US trade tariffs. These factors should support the BoJ's policy of eventually raising interest rates.

However, because most board members are cautious about raising rates too soon, a rate increase might be delayed until December. Supporting this view, I expect key data next week, like the Tokyo consumer price index, to show a strong rise of 2.5%, and for economic activity (like factory output and retail sales) to recover.

Central Bank Decisions and US-China Talks in Focus

The Federal Reserve is widely expected to cut its interest rate by another 0.25% on October 29th, following a similar cut last month. While the US economy looks decent and inflation is still a bit high, the Fed is shifting its focus because the risks are changing.

Price increases due to tariffs haven't been as severe as feared, giving time for factors like lower energy prices, slowing wage growth, and easing housing rents to help bring inflation down.

At the same time, the job market is starting to look more concerning, with many indicators suggesting an increasing risk of job losses. Because this trend points to both weaker economic growth and lower inflation in the future, the Fed feels that moving interest rates closer to a neutral level is the sensible thing to do.

The report on third-quarter economic growth (3Q GDP) is unlikely to be released next week because of the ongoing government shutdown.

The upcoming European Central Bank (ECB) meeting should be uneventful. Since the last meeting, economic data for the Euro area has been mixed: some surveys improved, but August's official data disappointed.

While September inflation briefly went above 2%, the new, important figures, such as the third-quarter GDP growth and the October inflation rate, will both be released on the day of the meeting itself.

With political risks calming down and officials signaling no rush, the ECB is expected to reaffirm its current stable position, with any potential decision on an interest rate cut pushed to December.

My opinion is that the October inflation rate will remain around 2%, and third-quarter economic growth will be a muted 0.1%, showing the economy is avoiding a recession despite global problems but is nowhere near a strong rebound.

Essentially, these data releases are expected only to confirm that the Eurozone economy is holding steady at a slow pace, so the ECB is unlikely to react to them strongly.

The Bank of Canada is expected to cut interest rates by 0.25% this week, even though recent reports showed job growth and inflation were a bit stronger than expected.

The central bank is under pressure to help Canada's economy, which has been severely damaged by US tariffs (since three-quarters of Canada's exports go to the US).

Additionally, Canadian consumers have very high debt levels, so the bank is likely stepping in to provide needed support.
